does: This paper investigates how different computational architectures for think-with-image reasoning in vision-language models affect their robustness to jailbreak attacks. The authors systematically evaluate four process designs: direct response generation, text-only prior turns, visual-state manipulation, and explicit external image-tool invocation across multiple models. They find that explicit image-tool interaction consistently reduces jailbreak attack success rates by approximately 30% on average. Crucially, the paper reveals this robustness emerges not from the semantic content of returned images or the textual traces of tool use, but through a distinct mechanism they term an image-tool safety vector frame, which maintains protective effects even when image outputs are manually corrupted or appear unsafe.
connects: This work builds on emerging research into multimodal reasoning and safety vulnerabilities in large vision-language models. It extends prior jailbreak studies that focused primarily on text-only systems to the more complex multimodal setting where visual reasoning introduces new attack surfaces and defense mechanisms. The paper responds to the broader question of how different inference paradigms affect model robustness, connecting to work on adversarial examples, prompt injection attacks, and safety mechanisms in large language models. The investigation of process design choices parallels recent inquiries into how reasoning chains and intermediate representations influence model behavior and safety across different architectures.
enables: This research opens pathways for designing safer vision-language model architectures by identifying that explicit tool invocation provides measurable robustness benefits independent of image semantics. It motivates deeper investigation into the nature of the image-tool safety vector frame itself and how similar protective mechanisms might be incorporated into other multimodal reasoning systems. The findings suggest future work on formalizing this safety emergent property, scaling these defenses across larger model families, and understanding whether analogous decoupling effects exist in other modalities or reasoning paradigms. The paper also positions image-tool interaction as a potentially underexplored defensive design space in multimodal AI safety, potentially influencing how production systems structure human-AI interaction workflows.
